region_of_origin: Amazon rainforest, South America (Bolivia, Brazil, Peru)
food_subgroup: Tree nuts — wild-harvested Brazil nut kernels (Bertholletia excelsa)

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for raw Brazil nut kernel; SNOMED CT coverage of individual raw nuts is limited.

serving.description: 1–2 nuts (maximum recommended daily intake due to selenium toxicity risk)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical safe serving is 1–2 Brazil nuts (approximately 5–10g), which provides the full recommended daily selenium intake; exceeding this regularly risks selenosis.

nutrient_source.title: Brazil Nuts Raw — USDA FoodData Central #170569
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central entry #170569 is an exact match for raw, unsalted Brazil nut kernels (Bertholletia excelsa). All macronutrient and micronutrient values are sourced directly from this entry.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Tree Nuts — Analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature for high-fat, low-digestible-carbohydrate nuts
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; peer-reviewed literature
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for raw Brazil nuts exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~25 is an analogous estimate based on the GI values of comparable high-fat, low-digestible-carbohydrate tree nuts (e.g., mixed nuts GI ~24, peanuts GI ~14–23) from the University of Sydney GI Database. The very high fat content (67.1g/100g) and low digestible carbohydrate content (~4.2g net carbs/100g) strongly attenuate postprandial glucose response. GL per 10g serving = (25 × 1.17g digestible carb per 10g) / 100 ≈ 0.3, which is negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: Negligible sodium (3mg/100g); magnesium and selenium support vascular function
hypertension.guidance: Brazil nuts are extremely low in sodium (3mg/100g) and provide magnesium (376mg/100g) which supports vascular smooth muscle relaxation; at the recommended 1–2 nuts/day (10g), sodium contribution is clinically negligible and the food is appropriate for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low GI (~25), negligible glycemic load, selenium supports GPx antioxidant defence
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 25 and a glycemic load of ~0.3 per 10g serving, Brazil nuts have a negligible impact on postprandial blood glucose; selenium (96–192mcg per 1–2 nuts) supports glutathione peroxidase antioxidant defence relevant to oxidative stress management in Type 2 Diabetes, making 1–2 nuts/day a clinically appropriate inclusion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat content (15.1g/100g) offset by very small recommended serving; selenium and vitamin E provide antioxidant benefit
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Brazil nuts have the highest saturated fat content of all common nuts (15.1g/100g); however, at the recommended 1–2 nuts/day (10g), SFA contribution is only ~1.5g, which is clinically insignificant, and the selenium (96–192mcg) and vitamin E (0.56mg per 10g) provide antioxidant selenoprotein support relevant to cardiovascular protection — strict adherence to the 1–2 nut daily limit is essential.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Selenium supports GPx and TrxR antioxidant selenoproteins; documented selenium deficiency in RA patients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Selenium at 96–192mcg per 1–2 nuts (175–349% RDI) supports glutathione peroxidase and thioredoxin reductase antioxidant selenoproteins relevant to joint oxidative stress management in rheumatoid arthritis, where serum selenium levels are documented to be 30–40% below normal; 1–2 nuts/day is the maximum safe intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Brazil nuts (Bertholletia excelsa) are large, cream-coloured tree nut kernels wild-harvested from the Amazon rainforest of Bolivia, Brazil, and Peru. They have a firm, crunchy texture and a rich, buttery flavour. Per 100g (USDA FDC #170569): 659 kcal, 14.3g protein, 67.1g fat (15.1g saturated — highest SFA of all common nuts), 11.7g carbohydrate, 7.5g dietary fibre, 3mg sodium. CRITICAL SAFETY NOTE: Brazil nuts contain approximately 1,917mcg selenium per 100g — the highest selenium concentration of any food. One nut (~5g) provides ~96mcg selenium (175% of the RDI of 55mcg). The maximum safe daily intake is 1–2 nuts (5–10g); regular consumption of more than 3–4 nuts/day risks selenosis (symptoms: alopecia, nail changes, garlic breath, neurological effects). GI ~25 (low), GL negligible per serving. Selenium supports glutathione peroxidase (GPx) and thioredoxin reductase (TrxR) antioxidant selenoproteins relevant to oxidative stress in Type 2 Diabetes, rheumatoid arthritis, and cardiovascular disease. Brazil nuts are almost entirely wild-harvested as commercial cultivation is impossible without the native orchid bee (Eulaema nigrita) for pollination. Available in Singapore at Cold Storage and NTUC FairPrice as a premium snack nut. Tree nut allergy is a critical contraindication. Dietary flags: vegan, vegetarian, halal, gluten-free, dairy-free.
common_user_queries: Brazil nuts how many per day safe
how many Brazil nuts can I eat per day

confidence_reason: All macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central #170569, an approved primary source with exact match to raw, unsalted Brazil nut kernels. Selenium value (1,917mcg/100g) is noted to have moderate natural variability depending on soil selenium content in the harvest region, but the USDA value is the standard reference. GI is an analogous estimate (confidence moderate) as no direct Brazil nut GI measurement exists in the University of Sydney database.
notes: 1. Selenium variability: The selenium content of Brazil nuts varies significantly by geographic origin and soil selenium levels (range reported in literature: ~100–2,500mcg/100g). The USDA FDC #170569 value of 1,917mcg/100g is the standard reference value used here. Clinical guidance should note this variability. 2. Monounsaturated fat (24.5g) and polyunsaturated fat (20.6g) values are sourced from USDA FDC #170569 fatty acid breakdown. 3. Water content (3.5g/100g) sourced from USDA FDC #170569. 4. Trans fat is confirmed zero per USDA FDC #170569. 5. Cholesterol is confirmed zero per USDA FDC #170569 — Brazil nuts are a plant food and contain no dietary cholesterol. 6. Vitamin A (0.0mcg RAE), vitamin D (0.0mcg), and vitamin B12 (0.0mcg) are confirmed zero per USDA FDC #170569 — these nutrients are naturally absent or below detection in raw Brazil nut kernels. 7. GI of ~25 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Brazil nuts exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. 8. SELENIUM TOXICITY WARNING: Maximum safe daily intake is 1–2 nuts (5–10g). Selenosis is a genuine clinical risk with regular overconsumption above 3–4 nuts/day. 9. Tree nut allergy is a critical contraindication. 10. This is a generic per-100g reference record; the default serving for clinical guidance is 10g (1–2 nuts).
